title: variant and type-safe alternatives

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for variant and type-safe alternatives; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A lookup returns optional<User> and the caller checks engagement before dereference rather than using a sentinel User. Your goal is to model and visit a small variant without unsafe type punning. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Standard algorithms; Class interface and invariants; Function templates; Composition and delegation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: optional represents presence or absence, variant represents one active alternative from a closed set, and visitation handles alternatives without unchecked type guessing. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: optional does not explain failure, variant can become valueless by exception in limited cases, get can throw, and adding an alternative requires visitor coverage. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: std::get automatically converts whichever variant alternative is active into the requested type.

includes: Represent a closed union of alternatives and handle every active case.
Model and visit a small variant without unsafe type punning.
excludes: optional does not explain failure, variant can become valueless by exception in limited cases, get can throw, and adding an alternative requires visitor coverage.

outcomes: Model and visit a small variant without unsafe type punning.

examples.title: variant and type-safe alternatives: defined case
examples.situation: A lookup returns optional<User> and the caller checks engagement before dereference rather than using a sentinel User.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A lookup returns optional<User> and the caller checks engagement before dereference rather than using a sentinel User.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: A variant has one active declared alternative; check or visit it, because std::get for the wrong alternative throws bad_variant_access.
examples.result: The defined result follows because A variant has one active declared alternative; check or visit it, because std::get for the wrong alternative throws bad_variant_access.

examples.title: variant and type-safe alternatives: boundary case
examples.situation: A parsed token is variant<Number,Name> and std::visit invokes exhaustive behavior for each alternative.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A parsed token is variant<Number,Name> and std::visit invokes exhaustive behavior for each alternative.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: optional does not explain failure, variant can become valueless by exception in limited cases, get can throw, and adding an alternative requires visitor coverage.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: std::get automatically converts whichever variant alternative is active into the requested type.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Model and visit a small variant without unsafe type punning.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: std::get automatically converts whichever variant alternative is active into the requested type.
misconceptions.correction: A variant has one active declared alternative; check or visit it, because std::get for the wrong alternative throws bad_variant_access.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: std::get automatically converts whichever variant alternative is active into the requested type.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model variant and type-safe alternatives: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
